_meta.mechanic: Build-a-Fraction (drag unit pieces into a target shape; commit by dragging the built assembly onto a numeral target). Self-correcting via gap/overhang. Numeral assembled from digit chips (no typing, no multiple choice).
_meta.replaces: Prior typed-answer / show-your-work problem bank has been replaced. This bank assumes the drag-and-build input modality decided on 2026-04-23.

problem_types_excluded.add_fractions: Out of scope. 3.NF and 4.NF.A do not include fraction addition (that is 4.NF.B.3). m02 (separate-num-den addition) therefore has zero direct coverage in this bank. Flagged for Barbara: if she wants m02 probed, we need to extend scope or accept that this bank cannot surface it.

notes_for_barbara: Every problem is solvable by drag-and-build plus numeral commit. No typed text. No multiple choice.

Real-world contexts used only from the 6-item fraction-native list (pizza/brownies/cookies, cooking, ruler inches, quarters, clock, music beats). About half the problems use honest abstract framing because no real context fit cleanly.
Shapes vary: bar, circle, number_line, set_of_objects — variation theory.
m02 is not probed because addition is outside this bank's scope; that is a conscious gap, not an oversight.

real_world_context.framing_text: Cut this pizza so 4 friends each get the same size slice. Then shade one slice for yourself.

problems.show_your_work_note: Learner drags 4 partition lines; lines snap to equal positions. Uneven attempts produce visible gap/overhang. Then drags one 1/4 shade-piece. Commit: build numeral 1/4 from digit chips.

real_world_context.framing_text: Split this bar into 3 equal parts. Shade two of them.

problems.show_your_work_note: Equal-partition check first. Then place two 1/3 shade pieces. Commit builds 2/3.

real_world_context.framing_text: Build one-fourth of this bar. You do not have a 1/4 piece — find another way.

problems.show_your_work_note: Goal denom 4 is intentionally absent from the palette to force equivalence reasoning. Two 1/8 pieces = 1/4. Commit = 1 over 4.

real_world_context.framing_text: Build three-fourths of this bar.

problems.show_your_work_note: Three 1/4 pieces. Commit = 3 over 4. (Framing kept abstract pending illustrated real-world targets.)

real_world_context.framing_text: Build five-eighths of this bar.

problems.show_your_work_note: Five 1/8 pieces. A learner holding m01 may reach for the wrong piece-size thinking 1/8 pieces are bigger. Commit = 5 over 8.

real_world_context.framing_text: Here are two bars. Build 5/4 — five pieces that are each one-fourth of a bar.

problems.show_your_work_note: Target is two bars (two wholes). Five 1/4 pieces = 1 full bar + 1/4 of the second bar. Probes acceptance of numerator > denominator. Commit = 5 over 4.

real_world_context.framing_text: A tray of 6 cookies, 2 with sprinkles. Build the fraction that names 'cookies with sprinkles'.

problems.show_your_work_note: Target pre-shown; learner assembles digit chips 2 and 6. Probes notation on a set-model whole.

real_world_context.framing_text: Place the 1/3 marker in the right spot on this number line from 0 to 1.

problems.show_your_work_note: Learner drags 3 ticks to partition [0,1] into thirds (material feedback if unequal). Then drags the '1/3' label to the first tick.

real_world_context.framing_text: Place 1/8 inch on this ruler.

problems.show_your_work_note: Partition into eighths first. m01 learner may misplace 1/8 farther right than 1/4 would be.

real_world_context.framing_text: This bar is one hour. Place 3/4 past the hour.

problems.show_your_work_note: Learner partitions into fourths, then drags '3/4' label three hops from 0.

real_world_context.framing_text: This number line goes from 0 to 3. Place 5/2.

problems.show_your_work_note: Partition halves across three wholes (6 ticks). Place 5/2 at the 5th tick. m03 learner will try to force it between 0 and 1.

real_world_context.framing_text: Place 5/6 on this line from 0 to 1.

problems.show_your_work_note: Six equal parts, then fifth tick. Tests partition accuracy at sixths (harder to eyeball than halves/fourths).

real_world_context.framing_text: Build a fraction the same size as 1/2, but using different pieces.

problems.show_your_work_note: Learner builds e.g. 2/4 or 4/8 on a second bar paired to a reference bar showing 1/2. Overhang/gap reveals mismatch. Commit = name what they built with digit chips.

real_world_context.framing_text: Build a fraction of this pizza the same size as 2/3, but using 1/6 slices.

problems.show_your_work_note: Four 1/6 slices equal 2/3. Same whole (same pizza), different denominator.

goal.note: set has 8 items, 4 highlighted; accept 4/8 or 1/2

real_world_context.framing_text: 8 cookies on a plate, 4 have chocolate chips. Build the simplest fraction that names the chocolate-chip cookies.

problems.show_your_work_note: Accept 4/8 or 1/2 at commit; log which was built (diagnostic for m04).

real_world_context.framing_text: Build a fraction the same size as 2/3 using sixths.

problems.show_your_work_note: Correct: 4/6. An m08 learner may try 3/4 (additive) but no 4ths in palette; any non-6ths attempt produces bar mismatch.

real_world_context.framing_text: Build a fraction the same size as 3/4 using eighths.

problems.show_your_work_note: Correct: 6/8. m08 learner tries 4/5 (not in palette); mismatch reveals error. Commit = 6 over 8.

real_world_context.framing_text: Build the whole bar using fourths.

problems.show_your_work_note: Four 1/4 wedges fill the whole. Commit = 4 over 4. Probes that a whole equals a fraction.

real_world_context.framing_text: Here are three identical bars. Build '3' using whole-bar pieces.

problems.show_your_work_note: Three 1/1 pieces fill all three bars. Commit = 3 over 1. Demonstrates whole number as fraction.

real_world_context.framing_text: Build 1/4 of one pizza and 1/8 of an identical pizza. Which piece is bigger?

problems.show_your_work_note: Two identical bars side by side. Visual shows 1/4 > 1/8. Commit = drag '>' between numerals.

real_world_context.framing_text: Build 3/8 on one bar and 3/4 on the other. Which is bigger?

problems.show_your_work_note: Same-numerator comparison. m01 learner may think 3/8 bigger; bars disprove.

real_world_context.framing_text: Build 2/3 on one bar and 3/4 on the other. Which is bigger?

problems.show_your_work_note: Different num AND denom. Learner can use benchmark (both > 1/2) or build equivalents. Visual bars resolve. Commit = '<'.

real_world_context.framing_text: Build 1/2 on one bar and 4/8 on the other. How do they compare?

problems.show_your_work_note: Equivalence surfaced as comparison. Commit = '='.

real_world_context.framing_text: Here is a small pizza and a LARGE pizza. Build 1/2 of the small one and 1/4 of the large one. Which piece is actually bigger?

problems.show_your_work_note: Two different-sized wholes. Targets m06 directly: 1/4 of a big pizza may exceed 1/2 of a small one. Commit by dragging a 'bigger' tag onto the winning circle.
